mainTopic: (continues) שתיית יין ושמן מזוקק · הפקדת חבית ושליחות יד
overview: (1) The Mishnah discusses deductions for wine and oil, and the Gemara analyzes the dispute between Rabbi Yehuda and the Sages regarding mixing dregs. (2) The Mishnah discusses the liability of a bailee who moves a deposited jar and breaks it, and the Gemara analyzes the concept of 'shlichut yad'. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Mishnah discusses deductions for wine and oil, and the Gemara analyzes the dispute between Rabbi Yehuda and the Sages regarding mixing dregs.
The Mishnah discusses the liability of a bailee who moves a deposited jar and breaks it, and the Gemara analyzes the concept of 'shlichut yad'. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: Challenge from Shmuel's ruling on profit limits
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges Rav Yehuda's calculation by citing Shmuel's ruling that one may not profit more than one-sixth from essential goods.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a difficulty against Rav Yehuda's calculation based on Shmuel's profit limit. It questions why Rav Yehuda's profit appears lower than the permitted maximum.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Shmuel rules that profit on essential goods cannot exceed one-sixth. Rav Yehuda's profit of 4 pitchers out of 48 is less than one-sixth. The challenge asks why he did not charge more to reach the full permitted profit.

phrases.aramaic: וְהָאָמַר שְׁמוּאֵל: הַמִּשְׂתַּכֵּר אַל יִשְׂתַּכֵּר יוֹתֵר עַל שְׁתוּת!
phrases.english: And Shmuel said: One who hires himself out, let him not hire himself out for more than a sixth!

steps.triggerLanguage: וְהָאָמַר שְׁמוּאֵל: הַמִּשְׂתַּכֵּר אַל יִשְׂתַּכֵּר יוֹתֵר עַל שְׁתוּת!

rashi.hebrew: והאמר שמואל המשתכר - בדברים שיש בהם חיי נפש:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that שמואל's ruling applies specifically to דברים שיש בהם חיי נפש, which is the category of items relevant to this case.

rashi.hebrew: אל ישתכר יותר על השתות - הא שתות ישתכר ולמה לא ישתכר רב יהודה עד שתות:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that since שמואל permits profit up to one-sixth, the Gemara questions why רב יהודה did not charge the full one-sixth.

tosafot.hebrew: אל ישתכר יותר על שתות - הא עד שתות משתכר ומסתמא רב יהודה היה מוצא שיתנו לו יותר ולמה הניח מלהרויח עד שתות:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות notes that since שמואל allows profit up to one-sixth, and רב יהUDA presumably could have found buyers willing to pay more, the Gemara asks why he left potential profit on the table.

steps.title: Resolution: barrel and sediment supplement the profit
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by noting that the barrel and sediment remain with Rav Yehuda, supplementing his profit.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא resolves the apparent under-profit by including non-liquid assets. Rav Yehuda retains the barrel and sediment, which add value to his earnings.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The barrel and sediment are not discarded; they have market value. When their worth is added to the 4 pitchers of liquid profit, the total reaches the permitted one-sixth limit.

phrases.english: There are barrels and sediments.

phrases.aramaic: אִי הָכִי נְפִישׁ לֵיהּ טְפֵי מִשְּׁתוּת?
phrases.english: If so, is his profit greater than one-sixth?
There is his labor and the cost of the tap.

rashi.hebrew: איכא גולפי ושמריא - משתכר הוא הקנקן והשמרים שלקח הכל בו' זוז:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that Rav Yehuda's profit is supplemented by the value of the barrel (קנקן) and the sediment (שמרים) which he retained, bringing his total earnings to the permitted amount.

rashi.hebrew: דמי ברזנייתא - שנותן דמים לחנווני למוכרן ומוציאן ומכריז עליו בחוצות. כרזנייתא לשון כרזא לשון אחר אומנות יש בנקיבת ברזא בחבית של חרס וצריך ליתן שכר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that דמי ברזנייתא refers to the cost of hiring a crier (כרזנייתא) to announce the sale, or alternatively, the fee for the specialized craft of drilling the barrel's tap (נקיבת ברזא).

steps.title: Challenge: absorption must occur even in saturated casks
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the Mishna's statement that old casks have no absorption deduction, arguing that some absorption must always occur.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא shifts to a new difficulty regarding the Mishna's exception for old casks. It objects that zero absorption is physically impossible.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Mishna exempts old casks from absorption deductions. The גמרא argues that even saturated casks must absorb some minimal amount, making a zero-deduction rule physically untenable.

phrases.aramaic: אִם הָיָה שֶׁמֶן מְזוּקָּק אֵינוֹ יוֹצִיא
phrases.english: If it was refined oil, he does not deduct
For them sediments, etc.
And isn't it impossible that it did not absorb!
Rav Naḥman said: With pitch-coated [casks] they taught.

phrases.aramaic: אַבָּיֵי אָמַר: אֲפִילּוּ תֵּימָא שֶׁלֹּא בִּמְזוּפָּפִין,
phrases.english: Abaye said: Even if you say not with pitch-coated [casks],
Since they are saturated — they are saturated.

rashi.hebrew: במזופפין - דכיון דישנים הם ונתבלעו בלעותם תו לא בלעי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רב נחמן's answer of מזופפין refers to old casks that have already absorbed their fill and therefore cannot absorb any more.

rashi.hebrew: כיון דטעון טעון - תו לא בלע:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that אביי's answer of כיון דטעון means that once a cask is saturated, it is considered fully saturated and absorbs no further.

tosafot.hebrew: במזופפין שנו - וא"ת כל שכן דבלע טפי כדאמרינן לעיל באתרא דמר חפו בכופרא דמייץ טפי וי"ל דהיינו חדש אבל ישן שכבר בלע אינו בולע יותר אי נמי התם ביין אבל הכא בשמן לא בלע כיון שהוא ישן:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses the difficulty that old casks might absorb more, as suggested by the earlier discussion of casks coated with lead, and answers that this applies only to new casks or to wine, whereas here we are dealing with old casks and oil, which do not absorb further once saturated.

steps.title: Abaye's analysis of Rabbi Yehuda's ruling on sediment
steps.stepSummary: Abaye analyzes Rabbi Yehuda's ruling to conclude that Rabbi Yehuda permits mixing sediment with oil, while the Sages prohibit it.
steps.whatsHappening: Abaye derives a broader halachic principle from Rabbi Yehuda's specific ruling on sediment deductions. He links the deduction requirement to the permissibility of mixing sediment.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Abaye reasons that if Rabbi Yehuda requires the buyer to accept a sediment deduction, he must permit mixing sediment. Conversely, the Sages' rejection of the deduction implies they prohibit mixing, as they would not force a buyer to accept prohibited goods.

phrases.english: Rabbi Yehuda says:

phrases.aramaic: אַף הַמּוֹכֵר שֶׁמֶן מְזוּקָּק לַחֲבֵירוֹ כׇּל יְמוֹת הַשָּׁנָה,
phrases.english: Even one who sells refined oil to his fellow all the days of the year,

phrases.aramaic: הֲרֵי זֶה מְקַבֵּל עָלָיו לוֹג וּמֶחֱצָה שְׁמָרִים לְמֵאָה.
phrases.english: this one accepts upon himself a log and a half of sediment for a hundred.
Abaye said: When you come to say,

phrases.aramaic: לְדִבְרֵי רַבִּי יְהוּדָה מוּתָּר לְעָרֵב שְׁמָרִים,
phrases.english: according to the words of Rabbi Yehuda it is permitted to mix sediment,
according to the words of the Sages it is forbidden to mix sediment.

rashi.hebrew: כשתמצא לומר - כשתדקדק ותדע מיצוי הדברים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that כשתמצא לומר means when you analyze and understand the precise implications of the matter.

rashi.hebrew: מותר לערב שמרים - המוכר שמן לחבירו סתם בעת שהוא מזוקק ששקעו ושקטו שמריו מותר לו לערב ולבלבל שמרים המשוקעות שיחזרו ויתערבו ויצאו עם השמן בתוך המדה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that מותר לערב שמרים means the seller is permitted to stir the settled sediment back into the oil so it is measured out together with the clear oil.

rashi.hebrew: אסור לערב שמרים - שבחזקת מזוקק לוקחו ממנו וזה מקלקלו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that אסור לערב שמרים means the seller is prohibited from stirring the sediment because the buyer purchased the oil with the חזקה that it was clear, and stirring would spoil it.

steps.title: Explanation: Rabbi Yehuda permits mixing, so the buyer accepts the deduction
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains why Rabbi Yehuda's permission to mix sediment leads to the buyer accepting the deduction.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara explains the logical link in Abaye's derivation. It shows why the buyer must accept the deduction under Rabbi Yehuda's view.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Since Rabbi Yehuda permits mixing, the seller argues he could have mixed sediment and sold it. Therefore, the buyer must accept the deduction as a standard part of the transaction.

phrases.aramaic: לְדִבְרֵי רַבִּי יְהוּדָה מוּתָּר לְעָרֵב שְׁמָרִים,
phrases.english: According to the words of Rabbi Yehuda, it is permitted to mix sediments,
And this is the reason that he accepts, for he says to him:
If I wish to mix for you –
Do I not mix for you?
Now too, accept.

steps.triggerLanguage: לְדִבְרֵי רַבִּי יְהוּדָה מוּתָּר לְעָרֵב שְׁמָרִים...

rashi.hebrew: והיינו טעמא דמקבל עליו - לוקח לוג ומחצה שמרים בשלא ערבו ונתנו לו מזוקק:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the buyer accepts the deduction because he purchased a measure of wine that included sediment, which was not mixed in but given separately as clarified wine.

rashi.hebrew: דא"ל - מוכר אי בעי לערובי כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the seller's argument is that if he wanted to mix the sediment into the wine, he could have done so according to רבי יהודה's opinion.

steps.title: Challenge: the buyer could have sold the mixed oil
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the seller's argument by noting that the buyer could have resold the mixed oil, so why accept the deduction?
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara raises a difficulty against the seller's argument. It questions why the buyer should accept the deduction.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The buyer argues that if the seller had mixed the sediment, the buyer could have resold it. Since the seller didn't mix it, the buyer is stuck with pure oil and shouldn't accept the deduction.

keyTerms.meaning: it could have been sold for me
what will I do with it

phrases.english: And let him say to him: If you had mixed it for him –
it would have been sold for me,
now what will I do with it?
By itself it is not sold for me!

phrases.aramaic: בְּבַעַל הַבַּיִת עָסְקִינַן – דְּנִיחָא לֵיהּ בְּצִילָא.
phrases.english: We are dealing with a homeowner – it is pleasant for him in the shade.
phrases.notes: The shade refers to the clarity of the oil, which is preferable for household use.

phrases.english: And let him say to him: Since you did not mix it for me –
you have renounced it to me!

rashi.hebrew: הוה מזדבן - כשהייתי מוכרו בחנות היה נמכר שמרים עם השמן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the buyer's claim 'הוה מזדבן לִי' means that if the seller had mixed the sediment, the buyer could have resold the mixture in his store, as mixed oil is marketable.

rashi.hebrew: בבעה"ב עסקינן - שהלוקח בעל הבית הוא ולקחו למאכל ביתו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the Gemara's response 'בבעה

tosafot.hebrew: לחודיה לא מזדבן - וא"ת ויתן לו מוכר ללוקח לוג ומחצה שמרים שיערב עדיין וימכור וי"ל דמיירי מתני' אפי' אין השמן בעין שלקחו מעט מעט ומכרו או אכלו:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses the difficulty that the seller could simply give the buyer the sediment to mix himself, and resolves it by explaining that the Mishnah's case assumes the oil is no longer in existence, having been sold or consumed bit by bit.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות raises a practical objection to the Gemara's assumption that the buyer couldn't resell the oil, which רש״י's explanation of the buyer's store context might imply, and resolves it by restricting the case to oil that is already gone.

steps.title: Resolution: Rabbi Yehuda does not accept renunciation
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by citing Rabbi Yehuda's general principle that renunciation is not presumed.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara resolves the difficulty by appealing to Rabbi Yehuda's broader halachic stance. It cites a Mishna in Bava Batza to prove his position.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rabbi Yehuda holds that one cannot presume renunciation. This is proven from a Mishna where he rules that the price determines what was sold, not the literal words. Therefore, the seller's failure to mix doesn't imply renunciation.

keyTerms.meaning: Rabbi Yehuda conforms to his reasoning
the money informs [the scope of the sale]

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי יְהוּדָה לְטַעְמֵיהּ, דְּלֵית לֵיהּ מְחִילָה.
phrases.english: Rabbi Yehuda according to his reasoning, that he does not have renunciation.
As we learned: He sold to him the yoke –
He did not sell to him the cattle.
He sold to him the cattle –
He did not sell to him the yoke.
Rabbi Yehuda says: The money informs.
How so? He said to him:
Sell to me your yoke for two hundred dinars –

phrases.aramaic: הַדָּבָר יָדוּעַ שֶׁאֵין הַצֶּמֶד בְּמָאתַיִים זוּז.
phrases.english: The matter is known that the yoke is not for two hundred dinars.
And the Sages say: The money is not proof.

rashi.hebrew: וחכמים אומרים אין הדמים ראיה - אע"ג דלא שוה כולי האי אחולי אחליה לוקח זה יתר הדמים ור"י לית ליה שיהא מוחל מן הסתם אלא אם כן פירש:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that according to חכמים, the buyer might voluntarily pay the excess price, but רבי יהודה holds that one does not presume מְחִילָה unless explicitly stated.

steps.title: Explanation: the Sages prohibit mixing, so the buyer rejects the deduction
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains why the Sages' prohibition on mixing leads to the buyer rejecting the deduction.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara completes Abaye's analysis by explaining the Sages' position. It shows why the buyer rejects the deduction under their view.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Since the Sages prohibit mixing, the buyer argues the seller couldn't have mixed the sediment. Therefore, the seller has no right to deduct for it, and the buyer rejects the deduction.

phrases.english: According to the words of the Sages, it is forbidden to mix sediments,

phrases.aramaic: וְהַיְינוּ טַעְמָא דְּלָא מְקַבֵּל – דַּאֲמַר לֵיהּ:
phrases.english: And this is the reason that he does not accept – that he says to him:

phrases.aramaic: אִי בָּעֵית לְעָרוֹבֵי מִי הֲוָה שְׁרֵי לָךְ?
phrases.english: If you wanted to mix, was it permitted for you?
Now too, we do not accept.

tosafot.hebrew: לדברי חכמים אסור לערב - וא"ת ונימא משום דאית ליה מחילה ולעולם מותר לערב וי"ל דמתניתין נמי מיירי אפי' כשהשמן עדיין בעין והתם אין שייך מחילה כיון שיכול לתקן כי יאמר לו קח השמרים וערב בשמן ולרב פפא דאמר דטעמייהו משום מחילה סבר דהשמן אינו בעין דאילו הוה בעין לא שייך ביה מחילה אי נמי סבר דאף ע"פ שהוא בעין שייך ביה מחילה כיון דמדדו מזוקק:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses the difficulty that the Sages' prohibition might be due to the buyer's forgiveness (מחילה) rather than a prohibition on mixing, and answers that the Mishna applies even when the oil is still present, where forgiveness is irrelevant because the buyer can simply take the sediment and mix it himself.

steps.title: Rav Pappa's reversal: the opposite is more logical
steps.stepSummary: Rav Pappa challenges Abaye's analysis, arguing that the opposite conclusion is more logical.
steps.whatsHappening: Rav Pappa raises a fundamental challenge to Abaye's entire analysis. He argues that the logic should be reversed.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Rav Pappa argues that the Sages' position should lead to permitting mixing, since they don't deduct. Rabbi Yehuda's position should lead to prohibiting mixing, since he deducts.

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב פָּפָּא לְאַבָּיֵי: אַדְּרַבָּה, אִיפְּכָא מִסְתַּבְּרָא!
phrases.english: Rav Pappa said to Abaye: On the contrary, the opposite is reasonable!
According to the words of the Sages, it is permitted to mix sediments,
And this is the reason that he does not accept –
For he says to him: From the fact that you did not mix for me,
You renounced it to me.

phrases.aramaic: לְדִבְרֵי רַבִּי יְהוּדָה אָסוּר לְעָרֵב שְׁמָרִים,
phrases.english: According to the words of Rabbi Yehuda, it is forbidden to mix sediments,
And this is the reason that he accepts – for he says to him:
If I wanted to mix –
It is not permitted for me to mix for you,
I do not accept.
Buying and selling at the same price, is he called a merchant?

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב פָּפָא לְאַבָּיֵי: אַדְרַבָּה...

rashi.hebrew: קבולי נמי לא מקבלת - עלך חסרון השמרים א"כ מה משתכר הרי השכר כולו בשמרים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that according to רבי יהודה, the owner would refuse to accept the deposit because the loss of the guards' wages would consume the entire profit, leaving him with no gain.

rashi.hebrew: זבון וזבין תגרא איקרי - בתמיה משל הוא לאמר לשוטה לקח בדמים וחזור ומכור בלא שכר ותיקרי תגר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י interprets the phrase זבון וזבין תגרא איקרי as a rhetorical question comparing the owner's situation to a foolish merchant who buys and sells without profit.

tosafot.hebrew: לדברי ר"י אסור - וא"ת ולימא מותר וטעמא דמקבל משום דלית ליה מחילה וי"ל דרב פפא סבר דצמד ובקר איכא הוכחה דלאו מחילה דמסתמא לא רצה ליתן מאתים זוז על הצמד בלא בקר אבל הכא לוג ומחצה שהוא דבר מועט מדלא עירב אחולי אחיל:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses why רבי יהודה's position implies a prohibition on mixing by distinguishing between a significant deduction (like the ox and yoke case) which proves no forgiveness, and a minor deduction (like the half-log here) which implies the owner forgave the loss by not mixing.

tosafot.hebrew: זבון וזבין תגרא איקרי - מיירי שלא היה מוצא למכור ביוקר יותר משלקחו לכך א"ל אדעתא דהכי קנית ממני שהיית יודע שאעכב לוג ומחצה כדי שארויח קצת:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the owner's deduction is justified because he bought the wine with the expectation of selling it at a higher price, and the deduction ensures he still makes a profit.

steps.title: Baraita on residue for buyers and depositors
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara cites a Baraita equating the status of buyers and depositors regarding residue.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a new source to clarify the status of residue. It asks what the Baraita means regarding buyers and depositors.
steps.deeperAnalysis: A Baraita teaches that buyers and depositors have the same status regarding residue. The Gemara asks if this means neither accepts residue, or both accept it.

phrases.aramaic: תַּנָּא: אֶחָד הַלּוֹקֵחַ וְאֶחָד הַמַּפְקִיד לִפְקָטִים. מַאי לִפְקָטִים?
phrases.english: It is taught: Whether one who buys or one who deposits with regard to residue. What is residue?

phrases.aramaic: אִילֵימָא כִּי הֵיכִי דְּלוֹקֵחַ לָא מְקַבֵּל פְּקָטִים,
phrases.english: If we say that just as the buyer does not accept residue,
so too the depositor does not accept residue –

phrases.aramaic: וְלֵימָא לֵיהּ: פְּקָטָךְ מַאי אֶיעְבֵּיד לְהוּ?
phrases.english: and let him say to him: What shall I do with your residue?

rashi.hebrew: אחד הלוקח ואחד המפקיד לפקטים - לוקח ומפקיד אחד הן לענין פקטים תורה אחת לשניהם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Baraita teaches that a buyer and a depositor are subject to the same halakhic rule regarding residue (פְּקָטִים).

rashi.hebrew: פקטים - פסולת הגרעינים הצפין על פני השמן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines פְּקָטִים as the spoiled grain residue that floats on top of oil.

rashi.hebrew: לוקח מקבל פקטים - אם מכר לו שמן סתם יקבל ממנו שמן עם פקטים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that a buyer who purchases oil without specifying otherwise is obligated to accept the residue (פְּקָטִים) along with the oil.

steps.title: Resolution: both buyers and depositors accept residue
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the ambiguity by stating that both buyers and depositors accept residue.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara clarifies the meaning of the Baraita. It states that both buyers and depositors accept residue.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Baraita means that just as a depositor accepts residue when his oil is returned, so too a buyer accepts residue with his purchase. The Gemara then challenges this by citing a Baraita that says buyers don't accept residue.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא כִּי הֵיכִי דְּמַפְקִיד מְקַבֵּל פְּקָטִים,
phrases.english: Rather, just as the one who deposits accepts residue,
the buyer too accepts residue.
And does the buyer accept residue?
And it was taught, Rabbi Yehuda says:

phrases.aramaic: לֹא אָמְרוּ שֶׁמֶן עָכוּר אֶלָּא לַמּוֹכֵר בִּלְבָד,
phrases.english: They did not say murky oil except for the seller alone,

phrases.aramaic: שֶׁהֲרֵי לוֹקֵחַ מְקַבֵּל עָלָיו לוֹג וּמֶחֱצָה שְׁמָרִים,
phrases.english: that the buyer accepts upon himself a log and a half of sediment,
without residue.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא כִּי הֵיכִי דְמַפְקִיד מְקַבֵּל פְּקָטִים...

rashi.hebrew: לא אמרו שמן עכור אלא למוכר - לא אמרו הפסד עכירת השמן אלא למוכר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the rule of שמן עכור (loss from residue) applies only to the seller, not the buyer.

rashi.hebrew: שהרי הלוקח מקבל עליו - לפחות ממאה לוגין לוג ומחצה מחמת שמרים לפיכך צריך ליתן לו שמן מזוקק בלא פקטים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that a buyer accepts a deduction of לוג ומחצה for שמרים (dregs), so he must receive pure oil without פקטים (residue).

tosafot.hebrew: לא אמרו שמן עכור אלא למוכר - פי' לא אמרו הפסד של עכירת פקטים אלא למוכר דכשנותן ללוקח שמן מזוקק אינו מחסר לו כלום ממאה לוג בשביל הפקטים כי אם לוג ומחצה בשביל השמרים מיהו עכירה לא שייכא כל כך בפקטים אלא בדבר המתערב כמו שמרים וי"ל דלא אמרינן הפסד של עכירת השמרים אלא למוכר שאם מוכר כשהוא מזוקק מקבל עליו לוקח בשביל השמרים לוג ומחצה והמוכר מרויח השמרים וכשהוא מוכר שמן עכור נותן מאה לוגין שלמים שמן עכור ומפסיד השמרים ע"י מה שמכר כשהוא עכור וכשמוכר מזוקק לא קרי הפסד לוקח אע"פ שמפסיד שמרים משום דבעה"ב ניחא ליה בצילא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the loss of residue applies only to the seller because the buyer only deducts for שמרים, and residue is more relevant to mixed substances like dregs, though the seller profits from dregs when selling pure oil but loses them when selling residue-heavy oil.

steps.title: Resolution: distinguishing between Tishrei and Nisan purchases
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the contradiction by distinguishing between purchases made in Tishrei vs. Nisan.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara provides a final resolution that reconciles the two Baraitot. It distinguishes between purchases made in different seasons.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The first Baraita refers to a purchase in Tishrei when oil is murky and residue is expected. The second Baraita refers to a purchase in Nisan when oil is refined and residue is not expected.

keyTerms.meaning: according to the measure of Tishrei

phrases.aramaic: לָא קַשְׁיָא: הָא דְּיָהֵיב לֵיהּ זוּזֵי בְּתִשְׁרִי וְקָא
phrases.english: Not difficult: this one where he gave him coins in Tishrei and he
takes from him in Nisan,
according to the measure of Tishrei.

phrases.aramaic: הָא דְּיָהֵיב לֵיהּ זוּזֵי בְּנִיסָן וְקָא
phrases.english: This one where he gave him coins in Nisan and he
takes from him in Nisan,
according to the measure of Nisan.

steps.triggerLanguage: לָא קַשְׁיָא: הָא דְיָהֵב לֵיהּ זוּזֵי בְּתִשְׁרֵי...

rashi.hebrew: כי מדה דתשרי - כשער הדמים של תשרי שהוא בזול וסתם שמנים עכורים וקיבל עליו פקטים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the first case involves a purchase in תשרי when oil prices are low and the oil is naturally murky, so the buyer accepts the residue (פקקים) as part of the standard measure.

rashi.hebrew: הא דשקל מיניה בניסן בשער של ניסן - ביוקר דדרך כל השמנים להיות מזוקקין בניסן והלוקח סתם והמוכר סתם אין מוכר אלא מזוקק:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the second case involves a purchase in ניסן when oil prices are high and the oil is naturally refined, so the buyer expects only refined oil and does not accept residue.

steps.title: The Mishna on a deposited jar
steps.stepSummary: The Mishna establishes the liability of a bailee who moves a deposited jar and breaks it, distinguishing between moving it for his own purposes vs. for its benefit, and whether a specific place was designated.
steps.whatsHappening: The sugya opens with the Mishna.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Mishna presents two scenarios: 1) No designated place: liable if broken in hand for his purposes, exempt if for its benefit or if broken after placing. 2) Designated place: liable if broken in hand or after placing for his purposes, exempt if for its benefit. The core distinction is between 'for his purposes' (liable) and 'for its benefit' (exempt), modulated by whether a place was designated.
steps.whatToRemember: The Mishna's liability hinges on 'לצורכו' vs 'לצורכה' and 'יחדו מקום' vs 'לא יחדו מקום'.
steps.confusionAlert: Note the exemption in the first clause when broken after placing, regardless of purpose. This is the crux of the Gemara's analysis.
steps.whyThisMatters: This Mishna is the primary source for the concept of 'שליחות יד' (misappropriation) and its liability.

phrases.aramaic: מַתְנִי׳ הַמַּפְקִיד חָבִית אֵצֶל חֲבֵירוֹ וְלֹא
phrases.english: MISHNA: One who deposits a barrel with his fellow and did not
designate for it owners a place,
and he moved it and it broke –
if from within his hand it broke,
for his needs – liable,
for its needs – exempt.
if from after he set it down it broke,
whether for his needs whether for its needs –
exempt. Designated for it the owners a place,
and he moved it and it broke –
whether from within his hand and whether from after he set it down,
for his needs – liable,
for its needs – exempt.

rashi.hebrew: מתני' לא יחדו לו הבעלים מקום - בבית שומר לאמר לו זויות זו השאילני:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase 'לא יחדו לה בעלים מקום' means the owner did not designate a specific corner in the guardian's house for the jar to remain.

rashi.hebrew: לצורכו - להשתמש בה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that 'לצורכו' refers to the guardian moving the jar for his own personal use or benefit.

rashi.hebrew: לצורכה - שהיתה במקום התורפה וקרובה להשתבר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines 'לצורכה' as moving the jar because it was in a vulnerable spot where it was at risk of breaking.

rashi.hebrew: אם משהניחה - שכילה תשמישו הושיבה במקום משתמר בין שטלטלה מתחלה לצורכה בין שטלטלה לצורכו פטור ובגמרא פריך מאי שנא לא יחדו מיחדו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that if the jar breaks after being placed down, the guardian is exempt regardless of the initial purpose of the move, because he fulfilled his duty by placing it in a secure spot, and notes that the Gemara will question the distinction between designated and non-designated places.

steps.title: The Mishna is Rabbi Yishmael
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara identifies the Mishna as following Rabbi Yishmael, who holds that returning a stolen item does not require the owner's knowledge.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara identifies the Tanna of the Mishna.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara links the Mishna's exemption (when the jar is placed back) to Rabbi Yishmael's principle that 'לא בעינן דעת בעלים' (we don't need the owner's knowledge). If the bailee places the jar back, it's considered 'returned' even without the owner's explicit consent, thus exempting him from liability for subsequent breakage.

keyTerms.meaning: one who steals a lamb [baraita source]

steps.whatToRemember: Rabbi Yishmael: 'לא בעינן דעת בעלים' for returning stolen items.
steps.confusionAlert: The connection is: placing the jar back = returning it. If Rabbi Yishmael says no knowledge is needed for return, then placing it back exempts the bailee.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the entire sugya's framework: liability vs. exemption hinges on whether the act counts as a 'return'.

phrases.english: GEMARA: Whose opinion is this?

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי יִשְׁמָעֵאל הִיא, דְּאָמַר: לָא בָּעִינַן דַּעַת בְּעָלִים.
phrases.english: It is Rabbi Yishmael, who says: We do not require the owner's knowledge.
As it is taught:

phrases.aramaic: הַגּוֹנֵב טָלֶה מִן הָעֵדֶר וְסֶלַע מִן הַכִּיס –
phrases.english: One who steals a lamb from the flock and a sela from the purse –
to the place from which he stole he shall return,
these are the words of Rabbi Yishmael.

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי עֲקִיבָא אוֹמֵר: צָרִיךְ דַּעַת בְּעָלִים.
phrases.english: Rabbi Akiva says: One requires the owner's knowledge.

rashi.hebrew: גמ' מני - הא דקתני רישא דאם משהניחה נשברה אף על פי שלצורכו נטלה מתחלה פטור דאמר משהחזירה הרי היא ברשות הבעלים כבתחלה ואינו עליה אלא כשומר חנם ופטור על אונסיה ואף על פי שלא הודיע לבעלים לומר נטלתיה והחזרתיה הויא השבה רבי ישמעאל היא כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי ישמעאל holds that returning the item to its original place constitutes a valid השבה even without the owner's knowledge, thereby reducing the bailee's status to that of a שומר חנם who is exempt from אונסין.
